name: Pope John Paul I Death
description: Pope John Paul I, Albino Luciani, died unexpectedly on September 28, 1978, just 33 days after his election. The official cause of death was a heart attack. However, the suddenness of his death, coupled with his perceived progressive views and intentions to reform the Vatican Bank, has fueled numerous conspiracy theories. These theories generally allege that he was poisoned or otherwise murdered due to his plans to expose corruption within the Vatican, particularly concerning its financial dealings and alleged connections to organized crime, such as the Mafia and Propaganda Due (P2) Masonic lodge. Some theories implicate high-ranking Vatican officials, bankers, or even foreign powers. Proponents point to inconsistencies in the initial Vatican accounts of his death, including conflicting reports about who found the body and what materials he was reading. Mainstream scholarship and the Vatican maintain that John Paul I died of natural causes, supported by medical evidence and witness testimonies. They attribute the conspiracy theories to sensationalism, misinformation, and a lack of understanding of the complexities of Vatican politics and finance. The official Vatican investigation concluded that there was no evidence of foul play and that the Pope's death was a result of a sudden heart attack, likely triggered by stress and overwork. Critics of the conspiracy theories also highlight the lack of concrete evidence to support the claims of murder or poisoning.
subtitle: Sudden death and conspiracy theories

claims.claim: John Paul I was murdered to prevent him from exposing Vatican corruption.
claims.evidence: Alleged inconsistencies in the official Vatican accounts of his death; his stated intentions to reform the Vatican Bank.
claims.criticism: The Vatican maintains a consistent narrative of death by natural causes; no forensic evidence supports poisoning or foul play.

claims.claim: The Mafia or P2 Masonic lodge were involved in his death due to his plans to investigate Vatican finances.
claims.evidence: Claims of Vatican Bank involvement in money laundering and connections to organized crime; John Paul I's intention to reform the Bank.
claims.criticism: No direct evidence links the Mafia or P2 to his death; the Vatican's investigation found no evidence of external involvement.

claims.claim: The initial Vatican accounts of his death were deliberately misleading to cover up the truth.
claims.evidence: Conflicting reports about who found the body, what he was reading, and the timeline of events.
claims.criticism: Minor discrepancies in initial reports can be attributed to shock and confusion following the unexpected death; these discrepancies do not constitute proof of a cover-up.

keyFigures.name: David Yallop
keyFigures.role: Author of "In God's Name," a prominent book promoting the murder theory.

keyFigures.name: John Cornwell
keyFigures.role: Author of "A Thief in the Night," initially investigated the conspiracies but concluded John Paul I died of natural causes.

mythologyConnections: The idea of a righteous leader being betrayed and killed for their beliefs
The theme of hidden power and corruption within religious institutions

timeline.event: Albino Luciani is elected Pope John Paul I.
Pope John Paul I is found dead in his bed.
David Yallop publishes "In God's Name," popularizing the murder conspiracy.
John Cornwell publishes "A Thief in the Night," initially examining the conspiracies but ultimately concluding in favor of natural causes.

sources.author: Lucien Gregoire
sources.work: Pope John Paul I: The Final Verdict
sources.title: Lucien Gregoire

sources.author: Jesús López Sáez
sources.work: Juan Pablo I: Crónica de un Papa
